coredata.dc:description: Ulcerative colitis (UC), a prevalent form of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), is characterized by the presence of chronic, nonspecific, and recurring inflammation in the intestine. Current therapies are insufficient in addressing the effective modulation of the intestinal oxidative stress-inflammation cycle, the repair of the intestinal mechanical barrier, and the regulation of gut microbiota and metabolites. There is a need for a comprehensive approach to orchestrate intestinal homeostasis more effectively. For this purpose, biocompatible antioxidant carbon dots nanozymes (GH-CDs) were synthesized based on glucose and D-histidine using a microwave-assisted method for the systematic management of intestinal inflammation. It was demonstrated that GH-CDs possessed excellent reactive oxygen/nitrogen species (ROS/RNS) scavenging capacities, thus modulating oxidative stress-induced damage and mitigating inflammation by inhibiting the TNF, MAPK, PI3K-Akt, NF-κB, and JAK-STAT signaling pathways. For in vivo experiments, GH-CDs have demonstrated both protective and therapeutic effects in mitigating colitis by reducing ROS levels, decreasing the infiltration of M1-type macrophages, suppressing the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines, and repairing intestinal mechanical and chemical barriers. Importantly, GH-CDs also influenced the gut microbiome to achieve a more beneficial state, which was accomplished by enhancing bacterial diversity and altering the microbial composition towards an anti-inflammatory type. Additionally, GH-CDs supported the biosynthesis of secondary bile acids and isoflavonoids. In conclusion, with excellent biocompatibility, GH-CDs will be a promising strategy for UC by comprehensively orchestrating intestinal homeostasis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and characterization of GH-CDs. (A) Preparation steps of GH-CDs. (B) The TEM image (scale bar: 20 nm) and HR-TEM image (scale bar: 2 nm) of GH-CDs. (C) Particle size distribution of GH-CDs. (D) The XRD pattern of GH-CDs. (E) The UV spectrum (black) and the fluorescence spectrum (red) of the GH-CDs. (F) Fluorescence emission spectra of GH-CD at different excitation wavelengths. (G) FT-IR spectra of the D-histidine, glucose, and GH-CDs. Wide-range (H) and C1 s (I), N1 s (J), and O1 s (K) XPS spectra of GH-CDs.

Antioxidant capacity of GH-CDs. (A) Schematic illustration of the ·ABTS+ radicals scavenging. The UV-vis absorbance spectra (B), clearance rates at 10 min (C), and clearance rates at different times (D) of ·ABTS + radicals after incubation with GH-CDs. (E) Schematic illustration of the ·O2 – radicals scavenging. The UV-vis absorbance spectra (F) and the clearance rates (G) of ·O2 – radicals after incubation with GH-CDs. (H) The ESR spectra of the ·O2 – radicals scavenging after incubation with GH-CDs of different concentrations. (I) Schematic illustration of the ·OH radicals scavenging. The UV-vis absorbance spectra (J) and the clearance rates (K) of ·OH radicals after incubation with GH-CDs. (L) The ESR spectra of the ·OH radicals scavenging after incubation with GH-CDs of different concentrations. (M) Schematic illustration of the ·DPPH radicals scavenging. The UV-vis absorbance spectra (N), the statistical chart of clearance rates (O) of ·DPPH radicals after incubation with GH-CDs. (P) Schematic diagram of the antioxidant capacity of GH-CDs. (n = 3).

The antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects of GH-CDs in vitro. Images (A; scale bar: 50 µm), flow cytometry (B), and its analysis (C) of DCFH-DA labeling. (D) Images of AM/PI staining (scale bar: 100 µm). Flow cytometry (E) and its analysis (F) of CD80 labeling. The levels of IL-1β (G), IL-6 (H), and TNF-α (I). (J) Immunofluorescence images of ZO-1 of each group in Caco2 cells (scale bar: 20 µm). (K) JC-1 staining fluorescence images in FHC cells (scale bar: 50 µm). (n = 3; * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001).

The biological mechanisms of GH-CDs by RNA-Seq analysis. (A) The heatmap of the whole DEGs. The volcano plot of DEGs (B) and GO enrichment analysis (C) between GH-CDs and LPS groups. Analysis of KEGG enrichment at the level of human disease (D) and environmental information processing (E). DEGs in the TNF (F), MAPK (G), NF-κB (H), JAK-STAT (I), and PI3K-Akt (J) signaling pathway. (n = 3).

In vivo bio-distribution of GH-CDs. (A) Fluorescence imaging of in vivo and ex vivo organs (H: heart, Li: liver, S: spleen, Lu: lung, K: kidney, and colon) after being injected with GH-CDs-Cy5.5 at various time points. Fluorescence intensity of healthy mice organs(B), colitis mice organs (C), and colons at 6 h (D). (n = 3; **p < 0.01).

The biocompatibility of GH-CDs. (A) Cell viability of RAW 264.7 cells after incubation with GH-CDs. (B) Cell viability of FHC cells after incubation with GH-CDs. (C) The hemolysis experiments of GH-CDs. (E) H&E staining images of the vital organs (scale bar: 200 µm) and their enlargements (scale bar: 100 µm). (F-O) Blood biochemical and routine blood indexes. (n = 3).

The therapeutic effect of GH-CDs on DSS-induced colitis. (A) Schematic diagram illustrating the experimental protocol for the preventive effect of the GH-CDs in colitis models. Body weight changes (B) and DAI scores (C) in each group throughout the study (n = 4). Photos of colons (D) and intestinal endoscopy (E) at the end of the experiment. (F) H&E staining of pathological sections in colons (scale bar: 100 µm). (G) Alcian blue staining images (scale bar: 200 µm). (H) Images of DCFH-DA (scale bar: 100 µm). (I) Statistical diagram of colon length (n = 4). MPO activity (J) and MDA content (K) in colon tissues (n = 3). Immunofluorescence images of TJPs (L), TUNEL (M), and CD80 staining (N) in the colons. The levels of IL-1β (O), IL-6 (P), and TNF-α (Q) in colons (n = 3; * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001).

Regulation of gut microbiota and metabolites in UC by GH-CDs. (A) The PCA plot of gut microbiota. (B) Venn diagram based on ASV data. The relative abundances of microbiota at the family (C) and genus (D) levels. The PLS-DA score plots (E) and permutation test plots (F) of metabolomics. (G) Volcanic maps of differential metabolites. (H) The relative abundance of differential metabolites of secondary bile acids. The relative quantification of ursodeoxycholic acid (I) and 7-ketodeoxycholic acid (J). (K) The GSEA diagram of isoflavonoid biosynthesis. The relative quantification of calycosin (L) and apigenin (M). (n = 4; * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01).

Preventive effect of GH-CDs on DSS-induced models. (A) Schematic diagram illustrating the experimental protocol for the preventive effect of the GH-CDs in colitis models. Body weight changes (B) and DAI scores (C) in each group throughout the study (n = 5). (D) Photos of the collected colons. (E) Photos of intestinal endoscopy. (F) H&E staining images of the colon of each group (scale bar: 400 µm) and their enlargements (scale bar: 100 µm). (G) Alcian blue staining of the colon from each group of mice (scale bar: 400 µm) and their enlargements (scale bar: 200 µm). (H) Statistical diagram of colon length. MPO activity (I) and MDA content (J) in colons (n = 3). The levels of RNA of IL-1β (K), IL-6 (L), and TNF-α (M) of colon tissues (n = 3; * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001).

The schematic representation of GH-CDs synthesis and therapeutic mechanisms on UC. The GH-CDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis approach based on glucose and D-histidine. a) Modulate intestinal immune homeostasis. The anti-oxidant activity of GH-CDs by scavenging ·O2 –, ·OH, and ·DPPH radicals (a1) and anti-inflammatory activity of GH-CDs by inhibiting the TNF, MAPK, PI3K-Akt, NF-κB, and JAK-STAT signaling pathways (a2). b) Repair intestinal mechanical barrier by safeguarding IECs from oxidative apoptosis and enhancing the expression of TJPs. c) Regulate gut microbiota and metabolites by increasing the probiotics, decreasing pathogens, and promoting the biosynthesis of secondary bile acids and isoflavonoids.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ulcerative colitis (UC), a prevalent form of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), is characterized by the presence of chronic, nonspecific, and recurring inflammation in the intestine. Current therapies are insufficient in addressing the effective modulation of the intestinal oxidative stress-inflammation cycle, the repair of the intestinal mechanical barrier, and the regulation of gut microbiota and metabolites. There is a need for a comprehensive approach to orchestrate intestinal homeostasis more effectively. For this purpose, biocompatible antioxidant carbon dots nanozymes (GH-CDs) were synthesized based on glucose and D-histidine using a microwave-assisted method for the systematic management of intestinal inflammation. It was demonstrated that GH-CDs possessed excellent reactive oxygen/nitrogen species (ROS/RNS) scavenging capacities, thus modulating oxidative stress-induced damage and mitigating inflammation by inhibiting the TNF, MAPK, PI3K-Akt, NF-κB, and JAK-STAT signaling pathways. For in vivo experiments, GH-CDs have demonstrated both protective and therapeutic effects in mitigating colitis by reducing ROS levels, decreasing the infiltration of M1-type macrophages, suppressing the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines, and repairing intestinal mechanical and chemical barriers. Importantly, GH-CDs also influenced the gut microbiome to achieve a more beneficial state, which was accomplished by enhancing bacterial diversity and altering the microbial composition towards an anti-inflammatory type. Additionally, GH-CDs supported the biosynthesis of secondary bile acids and isoflavonoids. In conclusion, with excellent biocompatibility, GH-CDs will be a promising strategy for UC by comprehensively orchestrating intestinal homeostasis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ulcerative colitis (UC), a prevalent form of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), is characterized by the presence of chronic, nonspecific, and recurring inflammation in the intestine [1]. Symptoms and manifestations of the disease vary according to its stage, with remission often presenting no discernible symptoms, while exacerbation may manifest as gastrointestinal symptoms and signs, such as abdominal pain, diarrhea, and mucopurulent bloody stool. In the advanced stages of the disease, serious complications such as intestinal perforation and toxic megacolon may occur [2,3]. The lack of healing capacity results in an elevated susceptibility to digestive diseases characterized by uncontrolled inflammation and malignant processes, such as colorectal cancer, which can have widespread effects on the body and significantly impact the quality of life [4]. Existing treatment modalities consist of traditional anti-inflammatory pharmaceuticals like 5-aminosalicylic acid (5-ASA), immunosuppressive agents such as thiopurine analogs and their metabolites, as well as biologics and small molecules. In cases where drugs are ineffective, proctocolectomy may be necessary [1,5]. The limitations of the aforementioned treatment options, including prolonged treatment duration, systemic toxicity resulting from high drug dosage and frequency of administration, suboptimal efficacy, and limited bioavailability due to inadequate drug targeting, underscore the imperative to transition away from current drug classes or delivery methods towards safer and more effective treatment strategies.

The etiology of UC has been the subject of extensive research, yet the precise causative factors remain incompletely understood. The prevailing perspective suggests that UC arises from a combination of endogenous factors, including genetic predisposition, and extrinsic stimuli, such as dietary and chemical influences [6–8]. Key contributors to the initiation and advancement of intestinal inflammation include oxidative stress, dysregulated immune responses, compromised epithelial barriers, and ecological dysregulation [1,9–11]. In physiological circumstances, reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated by the body interact with the antioxidant system to regulate the redox balance. However, inflammation may lead to an overproduction of ROS that surpasses the scavenging capabilities of the body, leading to oxidative stress damage [12,13]. In individuals with UC, heightened levels of ROS and prolonged oxidative stress within the intestinal tissues can lead to the development of mucosal ulcers, hasten the progression of disease foci, and intensify inflammatory and immune reactions, thereby worsening pathological changes such as apoptosis of intestinal epithelial cells (IECs), impairment of the intestinal mucosal barrier, and dysbiosis of the gut microbiota [14]. Consequently, the implementation of antioxidant therapy presents a viable strategy for managing IBD by effectively inhibiting and neutralizing the excessive production of ROS to mitigate inflammation [15,16].

The intestinal barrier serves as the primary defense mechanism separating the intestinal tract from the external environment, effectively preventing pathogen infiltration and toxin absorption. This barrier is comprised of an intricate structure, including the surface mucus layer, epithelial cell layer, and lower non-epithelial mucosal cells, predominantly leukocytes with diverse regulatory and effector capabilities. The intestinal barrier comprises mechanical, chemical, immune, and biological components [17]. Numerous studies have demonstrated that impairment of the intestinal barrier occurs several years before the onset of IBD symptoms. Furthermore, throughout the progression of the disease, ongoing disruption of barrier function and dysregulation of the immune system contribute to gradual tissue damage and the development of clinical IBD [18,19]. Intestinal epithelial cells and tight junction proteins (TJPs) are important components of the intestinal mechanical barrier, which serves to isolate pathogens and interact with other immune cells [20]. The intestinal mucus layer, a significant component of the intestinal chemical barrier, is primarily composed of mucin secreted by goblet cells. Alterations in the thickness and composition of the mucus layer are associated with the severity of UC [21]. The maintenance of a healthy intestinal microbiota is crucial for modulating the host's immune homeostasis and preventing the infiltration of pathogens into the body. Disruption of the microbial barrier function can result in the colonization of pathogenic microorganisms and subsequent inflammation [22]. Consequently, the restoration of intestinal barrier integrity is a key component in the therapeutic management of UC.

The exceptional catalytic efficiency and substrate specificity exhibited by natural antioxidant enzymes render them highly suitable catalysts for biomedical purposes. Nonetheless, the majority of these enzymes are protein-based, inherently unstable, expensive, arduous to produce, and challenging to scale up for mass production. Consequently, the drawbacks associated with natural enzymes have prompted researchers to explore artificial nanozymes with antioxidant enzyme-like activity, which offer the advantages of facile synthesis, cost-effectiveness, and enhanced stability, thus garnering significant interest. Up to now, the enzyme-like activity of nanozymes has been broadly studied and used in the prevention and treatment of various diseases [23–26]. Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel category of luminescent zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with small size, typically comprised of an amorphous carbon atom center of sp2/sp3 and a shell containing numerous O/N/S functional groups or polymer chains [27,28]. There are two main strategies for carbon dots to achieve scavenging of ROS, one of which is to utilize the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on their surfaces to adsorb oxygen radicals to form adducts and consume them by electron transfer [29,30]. The other is by doping metals or heteroatoms for the purpose of transferring electrons [31]. Compared with other nanozymes, carbon-dots nanozymes show easy synthesis steps, good biocompatibility, and good solubility [28]. In the field of biomedical applications, carbon-dots nanozymes also show good potential for antibacterial, antiviral, antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, tissue damage repair, new tumor therapy, etc [28,29,32,33].

In this study, carbon-dots nanozymes (GH-CDs) were produced through a microwave-assisted technique employing glucose and D-histidine as precursors, both vital small molecules in biological processes. The GH-CDs demonstrate remarkable antioxidant capabilities attributed to the presence of oxygen-rich groups on their surface, which proficiently scavenge reactive oxygen and nitrogen species (ROS/RNS) to alleviate oxidative stress injury. Additionally, the GH-CDs demonstrate anti-inflammatory properties by inhibiting macrophage phenotype transformation to pro-inflammatory M1, lowering the pro-inflammatory cytokine levels, and eliciting other anti-inflammatory effects. Furthermore, GH-CDs possess a significant reparative impact on the integrity of the intestinal mechanical and chemical barriers and exert regulatory effects on the intestinal microbiota and metabolism, demonstrating promising potential in the management of UC (Scheme 1
).

Glucose, D-Histidine, 1,1-Diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), and 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were commercially available from Shanghai Aladdin. ABTS assay kit, JC-1 assay kit, and dihydroethidium (DHE) probe were acquired through Beyotime. The 2,7-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH) probe was acquired from Solarbio (Beijing, China). Lipopolysaccharides (LPS) were obtained through Sigma-Aldrich. Dextran sulfate sodium (DSS) was purchased from MP Biomedicals. The myeloperoxidase (MPO) and malondialdehyde (MDA) assay kits were commercially available through Nanjing Jiancheng Co., Ltd.. Deionized water was used to prepare all aqueous solutions.

To prepare GH-CDs, 40 mM of glucose, and 40 mM of D-histidine were first mixed in 20 mL deionized water under ultrasonic conditions for 15 min. The solution was then heated and reacted in a microwave oven (700 W) for 20 min, then removed and re-dissolved with water after natural cooling. Subsequently, the product underwent centrifugation for 10 min with a speed of 10,000 rpm. The collected supernatant was immersed in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 3500 Dalton (Da) for 72 h to obtain a product of uniform size. Finally, the obtained GH-CDs were freeze-dried and preserved.

Images of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM were captured using a FEI Tecnai G2 F30 microscope. The Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZSE was used to determine zeta potential. The SHIMADZU (UV-2700) and HITACHI (F-4700) Spectrophotometer were respectively used for Ultraviolet-Visible (UV-Vis) absorption and fluorescence spectrum. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were determined through Nicolet 5700 and Thermo Fisher ESCALAB Xi+, respectively. The VISQUE® InVivo Smart-LF system was used for In vivo and Ex vivo imaging of mice. Electron Spin Resonance (ESR) spectra were measured on CIQTEK EPR200M.

NBT assay: Different concentrations of GH-CDs (10, 20, 50, 100, and 200 μg/mL) were added to riboflavin (20 μM), nitro blue tetrazolium (NBT, 0.05 mM), and L-met (13 mM) in PBS, whose optical density at 560 nm and absorption spectrum were measured after LED irradiation for 2 min.

TMB assay: Fenton's reagent (10 μM Fe2+ and 50 μM H2O2) was mixed to generate the hydroxyl radical (·OH) which could oxidize TMB to oxTMB and incubated with TMB (0.3 mM) and GH-CDs (20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 μg/mL), whose optical density at 645 nm and absorption spectrum were measured after 10 min for reaction.

·ABTS+ assay: Firstly, the ·ABTS+ radicals were obtained by adding ABTS solution into oxidizing agents to fully react overnight. Then, GH-CDs with proper concentrations (1, 2, 4, 8, and 16 μg/mL) were mixed with ·ABTS+ radicals for several minutes, and then the optical density at 734 nm was measured.

·DPPH radical assay: The ·DPPH (125 μM) in ethanol were combined with GH-CDs of various concentrations (10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 μg/mL) for 30 min. Subsequently, their optical density at 517 nm and absorption spectrum were measured.

ESR assay: i) The ·O2 – radicals were prepared by mixing DTPA (1 mM), xanthine oxidase (XOD, 2.45 mM), and xanthine (XA, 0.4 mM) in PBS, and then BMPO (5 mg/mL) was added as the free radical trapping agent. Their ESR spectra were recorded after the addition of GH-CDs at 37 °C for 10 min. ii) The ·OH radical was captured by mixing DMPO (100 mM), FeSO4 (50 μM), and H2O2 (10 μM). The ESR spectra were recorded after adding GH-CDs of different concentrations for 3 min.

The culture medium used for mouse mononuclear macrophages (RAW 264.7) and human colon epithelial cells (FHC) was DMEM enriched with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1 % penicillin/streptomycin. The MEM medium containing 20 % FBS and 1 % penicillin/streptomycin was provided for human colorectal adenocarcinoma cells (Caco-2). The culture conditions maintained were 37 ℃ with 5 % CO2.

EDC/NHS crosslinking agents were utilized to achieve the covalent coupling of GH-CDs with Cy5.5. Initially, 100 μL of N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N’-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 10 mg/mL) and 200 μL of N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 10 mg/mL) were mixed and activated for 30 min. Subsequently, 1 mL of GH-CDs (5 mg/mL) was added, stirring the mixture for 1 h. After that, 2 mL Cy5.5 (0.25 mg/mL) was added for a complete reaction over 24 h. Finally, the mixture was dialyzed for 24 h using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 3500 Da to remove any unbound reactants.

GH-CDs labeled by Cy5.5 were used for cellular uptake investigation. RAW 264.7 cells were plated on glass slides for 24 h and then were incubated with GH-CDs-Cy5.5. Quantification via flow cytometry and the capture of fluorescence microscopy images were conducted following the co-incubation of cells with GH-CDs-Cy5.5 at various time points.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects of GH-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Antioxidant experiments: RAW 264.7 cells were plated on culture plates overnight, followed by incubation with GH-CDs for 8 h. To induce oxidative stress damage, rosup/H2O2 (rosup: added according to the instructions; H2O2: 500 μM) was added to the culture for a duration of 4 h. The MTT method was utilized to determine the relative cell viability. 2,7-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA, 20 μM) and dihydroethidium (DHE, 1 μM) were utilized to evaluate the intracellular ROS scavenging ability. Additionally, calcein AM (AM, green) and propidium iodide (PI, red) were utilized to study its protective effect. After completion of cell staining, flow cytometry quantification, and fluorescence microscopy photographs were performed.

Anti-inflammatory experiments: We seeded RAW 264.7 cells into culture plates overnight, followed by incubation with GH-CDs for 8 h, and then LPS (1000 ng/mL) was added for 12 h to induce inflammation. After co-incubation RAW 264.7 cells were stained with CD80 (20 μM) for 0.5 h to assess macrophage polarization and real-time fluorescence PCR (RT-qPCR) was used to quantify the levels of pro-inflammatory factors. After completion of cell staining, flow cytometry quantification, and fluorescence microscopy photographs were performed.

JC-1 staining: FHC cells were seeded into tissue culture plates overnight, followed by incubation with GH-CDs for 8 h, and then H2O2 (500 μM) was added for 4h to induce mitochondrial damage. Fluorescence imaging was performed after JC-1 staining for 0.5 h.

ZO-1 staining: Caco-2 cells were seeded into glass slides for 3 weeks, followed by incubation with GH-CDs for 8 h, and then LPS (1000 ng/mL) was added for 24 h to induce inflammatory injury. Cells were incubated overnight at 4 ℃ with ZO-1 monoclonal antibody for staining, followed by fluorescence imaging.

All animal studies were conducted with approval and oversight from the Animal Ethics Committee at Wenzhou Medical University. Female C57BL/6 mice were acquired from GemPharmatech Co., Ltd., while female Kunming mice were sourced from the Experimental Animal Center at Wenzhou Medical University. The mice were maintained in an environment with temperatures ranging from 22 to 25 °C, relative humidity of 65 ± 5 %. They were provided with standard feed and had unrestricted access to drinking water.

RAW 264.7 and FHC cells were plated in plates overnight and co-incubated with CH-CDs of different concentrations for 24 h or 48 h, whose cell viability was determined by MTT assay. We divided the mice into the control group or the GH-CDs group at random. Mice in the GH-CDs group were intraperitoneally injected with GH-CDs (30 mg/kg/d) for 7 d, while those in the control group received no treatment. These mice were euthanized seven days later. Subsequently, pathological findings of vital organs, blood routine, and blood biochemical indexes were used for biocompatibility evaluation.

GH-CDs labeled by Cy5.5 were used for in vivo biodistribution studies. We divided the C57BL/6 mice into the control group or the colitis group at random. The colitis group was provided with drinking water containing 2.5 % DSS for 7 d to induce colitis. Subsequently, the in vivo and ex vivo distribution imaging of GH-CDs was recorded at various time points after intraperitoneal injection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo preventive and therapeutic effects of GH-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: We assigned female C57BL/6 mice to one of five groups at random: the control group, the DSS group, the 10 mg/kg/d GH-CDs group, the 20 mg/kg/d GH-CDs group, and the 100 mg/kg/d 5-ASA group.

To evaluate the preventive effect of GH-CDs, colitis modeling was performed by drinking water with 2.5 % DSS for 7 d, during which GH-CDs groups were given intraperitoneal injections every other day, while the 5-ASA group was given gavage every other day. For assessing the therapeutic effect, mice in the GH-CDs group and the 5-ASA group were treated for 5 days after the completion of colitis modeling. Daily measurements of body weight and the disease activity index (DAI) were taken and recorded throughout the experiment. Following euthanasia, the colon and cecum were excised and collected for pathological examination, qPCR analysis, and other assessments to determine the in vivo therapeutic and prophylactic effects of GH-CDs.

All results were subjected to statistical analysis using GraphPad Prism. The statistical significance was assessed through student’s t-tests or one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001). The experimental data were presented in the form of mean ± standard deviation (SD).

The GH-CDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis approach based on Glucose and D-Histidine (Fig. 1
A). As illustrated in Fig. 1 B, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images revealed the morphology of GH-CDs, indicating that they were evenly dispersed and exhibited a uniform size distribution of approximately 3.44 ± 0.75 nm (Fig. 1 C). Additionally, the zeta potential of the GH-CDs was measured at −10.93 ± 0.58 mV, as shown in Figure S1 . High-resolution TEM images (inset of Fig. 1B) indicate that the GH-CDs exhibit a lattice spacing of about 0.23 nm. This spacing aligns with the (100) plane of graphite, implying that the GH-CDs likely possess a structure akin to graphitic crystals. To investigate its stability, we performed TEM imaging of the GH-CDs after 14 days of synthesis, which showed that the GH-CDs exhibited similar dispersibility and particle size ( Figure S2
A). As shown in Fig. 1 D, X-ray Diffraction (XRD) patterns revealed that the GH-CDs displayed a distinct diffraction peak at 2θ of about 20°, indicating the presence of a structure close to graphene and the disorder of carbon dots. The UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques were employed to gain insight into the optical properties of GH-CDs. Upon examination, the GH-CDs were observed as an absorption peak at approximately 270 nm, and the fluorescence spectra, when excited at 370 nm, revealed a peak emission centered at 442 nm (Fig. 1 E). Additionally, as the excitation wavelength varied from 350 to 390 nm, the peak emission shifted from 436 to 452 nm (Fig. 1 F), which exhibited a distinct dependence on the excitation wavelength. FT-IR and XPS techniques were employed further to investigate the structure and composition of the GH-CDs. FTIR analysis revealed several key peaks: a broad peak at 3350.8 cm−1 associated with O − H/N − H stretching, a peak at 2930.4 cm−1 relative to C − H bending vibration, a peak at 1616.1 cm−1 indicative of C = O groups, a peak at 1384.7 cm−1 related to C − H bending vibration, and a peak at 1081.9 cm−1 representing C − N stretching vibration (Fig. 1 G). GH-CDs retained some functional groups of glucose as well as certain nitrogen-containing structures associated with D-histidine, which may play a key role in the radical scavenging activity of GH-CDs. The results of XPS indicated the GH-CDs were constituted with C, O, and N elements (Fig. 1 H). The C1s spectrum was resolved into four distinct peaks at 288.8, 287.5, 286.0, and 284.6 eV, which were respectively assigned to several carbon species: N − C = O, C = O, C − N/C − O, and C − C/C = C (Fig. 1 I). Three curves with peaks of 400.9, 400.0, and 398.8 eV in the N1s spectrum were respectively associated with graphitic, pyrrolic, and pyridinic nitrogen (Fig. 1 J). The O1s spectrum was resolved into two curves with 532.2 and 531.1 eV peaks, representing C − O and C = O, respectively (Fig. 1 K). To further investigate the surface structure of GH-CDs, we conducted ahydrogen nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) analysis ( Figure S3 ). In the 1H NMR spectrum of GH-CDs, the two peaks at 7.64 ppm and 6.88 ppm can be attributed to protons on the aromatic and alkene groups, respectively. The peaks between 4.00 and 3.00 ppm may be due to hydrogen atoms on carbons adjacent to the hydroxyl group and oxygen. The peaks in the range of 2.97 to 1.92 ppm are likely caused by hydrogens on carbons adjacent to thecarbonyl group and nitrogen, while the peak at 1.83 ppm corresponds to protons on carbons adjacent to the alkene. These results suggested that GH-CDs were abundant functional groups, thereby elucidating their remarkable water solubility and antioxidant properties.

Given the critical role of ROS/RNS in UC, we assessed the capability of GH-CDs as scavengers of ROS/RNS (·ABTS+, ·O2 –, ·OH, and ·DPPH). Initially, we determined the overall antioxidant capacity through the ABTS method (Fig. 2
A-D). In this experiment, the green ·ABTS+ radicals were prepared by fully reacting a mixture of ABTS and oxidants overnight. Then different concentrations of GH-CDs and ·ABTS+ were incubated for 10 min, and the green color of the mixture was gradually weakened depending on the concentration which indicated the excellent overall antioxidant capacity of GH-CDs.

To further explore the ROS scavenging properties of GH-CDs, we investigated the scavenging ability of two important radicals, ·O2 – and ·OH, separately. The ·O2 – removal capacity was determined by NBT and ESR methods. Riboflavin generated ·O2 – under aerobic conditions that reduced NBT to dark purple NBT-formazan (Fig. 2 E), whereas GH-CDs exhibited SOD-like activity that reduced the production of colored NBT-formazan in a concentration-dependent manner (Fig. 2 F-G). Besides, we utilized the XA/XOD system to generate ·O2 – and added BMPO as a trapping agent, which produced a BMPO/·OOH spin adduct. The incorporation of GH-CDs led to a notable decrease in the intensity of radical signaling, allowing for a more intuitive visualization of ·O2 – removal (Fig. 2 H). The ·OH radicals were another important ROS with strong oxidative properties, so effective removal of ·OH also effectively prevented cells from oxidative stress injury. We generated ·OH by the Fenton reaction (Fe2+/H2O2), and then used the change of ESR signal intensity and TMB assay to reflect the ·OH removal efficiency (Fig. 2 I). We exploited the fact that TMB substrates are oxidized to blue under the influence of ·OH and observed that the liquid showed less blue color as the concentration of GH-CDs increased, suggesting that GH-CDs possessed concentration-dependent ·OH scavenging ability (Fig. 2 J-K). Moreover, DMPO worked as a trapping agent to trap ·OH and produced a DMPO/·OH spin adduct. In comparison to the control, adding GH-CDs decreased the ESR signal intensity, indicating it effectively scavenged ·OH (Fig. 2 L). The ·DPPH was a stable nitrogen-centered radical that appeared purple, which faded significantly when mixed with low concentrations of GH-CDs (Fig. 2 M), and its absorption spectrum decreased significantly (Fig. 2 N-O). The excellent RNS scavenging ability of GH-CDs was demonstrated in this experiment. Taken together, the above results manifested that GH-CDs could scavenge ROS/RNS (Fig. 2 P), which is promising for colitis therapeutics. Moreover, the stability of enzyme activity in materials is crucial for their practical applications. To this end, we compared the radical scavenging ability of GH-CDs at the same concentration after being stored for different durations, which indicated that the GH-CDs maintained the same enzymatic activity characteristics even after 14 days ( Figure S2
B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects of GH-CDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Beyond their role in scavenging ROS, macrophage phenotypes are crucial to the development of numerous inflammatory diseases. Within inflamed tissues, macrophages predominantly exhibit the M1 phenotype, which contributes to inflammation through the release of various pro-inflammatory cytokines. Consequently, targeting the reduction of M1 phenotype macrophages represents a promising approach for the treatment of UC. To explore this possibility, the effect of GH-CDs on macrophage polarization was examined in RAW 264.7 cells treated with LPS. We performed flow cytometry by labeling with antibodies against the M1 marker CD80 and indicated that GH-CDs significantly reduced the proportion of M1 phenotype after LPS treatment (Fig. 3 E-F). Meanwhile, we examined the levels of the pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β, which were significantly higher in LPS-treated RAW264.7 cells and significantly lower in the GH-CDs pre-treated group (Fig. 3 G-I). Thus, GH-CDs could inhibit macrophage polarization towards pro-inflammatory M1 type and scavenge pro-inflammatory cytokine expression, resulting in an anti-inflammatory effect.

Caco-2 cells could be cultured to differentiate into a structurally and functionally similar human intestinal epithelium with polarity. To this end, we used caco-2 cells to mimic the intestinal epithelial layer, LPS intervention to model intestinal epithelial barrier injury, and TJP ZO-1 expression detected by immunofluorescence. Compared with the control group, ZO-1 fluorescence intensity was significantly attenuated in the LPS group, whereas it was significantly improved in the GH-CDs group and showed better results at high concentrations (Fig. 3 J). Mitochondria are both the primary site of intracellular ROS production and the primary target organ for ROS attack and damage. Excessive ROS can induce the mitochondria permeability transition pore open, resulting in mitochondrial swelling and rupture, and releasing cytochrome C, ultimately causing mitochondrial dysfunction and apoptosis. In this study, we used H2O2 to induce mitochondrial damage in human colon epithelial cells (FHC), which was analyzed by incubation with a fluorescent probe, JC-1. In the H2O2 group, the cellular mitochondria were depolarized and less electronegative, while intracellular JC-1 was present as green-fluorescent monomers, whereas in the GH-CDs pre-incubation group there was a decrease in the monomeric form, and an increase in the red fluorescent JC-1 multimeric form (Fig. 3 K). These results indicated that GH-CDs could protect the intestinal barrier by restoring the levels of TJPs and inhibiting mitochondrial damage and apoptosis in intestinal epithelial cells, which illustrated the promise of GH-CDs as a treatment modality for UC.

Through an extensive RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) analysis of LPS-stimulated macrophages that were treated with GH-CDs, we gained a valuable understanding of the intricate anti-inflammatory mechanisms associated with GH-CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 4
A, the overall differentially expressed genes (DEGs) between the three groups were compared. Volcano plot presented in comparison to the LPS group, GH-CDs up-regulated 199 genes and down-regulated 541 genes (Fig. 4 B), which showed a great difference between the two groups. To further explore the biological functions and major enrichment pathways of the differential genes, the Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) and the Gene Ontology (GO) enrichment analysis were conducted. The GO enrichment analysis demonstrated that DEGs were predominantly associated with various immune-related biological processes, including inflammatory response (GO: 0006954), immune system process (GO: 0002376), innate immune response (GO: 0045087), and immune responses (GO: 0045087), suggesting that GH-CDs may have positive immunomodulatory and anti-inflammatory effects (Fig. 4 C). Meanwhile, the KEGG enrichment analysis in human diseases showed that GH-CDs treatment affected gene expression in some autoimmune diseases, such as IBD, rheumatoid arthritis, and graft-versus-host disease, compared to the LPS group (Fig. 4 D). Besides, KEGG enrichment analysis demonstrated that the anti-inflammatory properties of GH-CDs were closely related to several signaling pathways, in particular the TNF, MAPK, PI3K-Akt, JAK-STAT, and NF-κB signaling pathways (Fig. 4 E). The differentially expressed genes in Fig. 4 F suggested that GH-CDs might lower the levels of the inflammatory cytokines IL1β, IL6, and vascular cell adhesion molecule 1 (VCAM-1), a significant element of leukocyte recruitment to the inflamed bowel [34], by affecting the TNF pathway. The inhibitory impact of GH-CDs on the p38 MAPK pathway was reflected by decreasing the expression of the Mapk13 (p38) gene and its downstream Mapkapk3 (MAPKAPK) gene (Fig. 4 G). Activation of classical NF-κB by IL1β stimulated the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines, including IL1β and TNF-α, triggering an inflammatory response, and at the same time, these cytokines in turn activate classical NF-κB, creating a positive feedback loop. In contrast, in comparison to the LPS group, GH-CDs lowered the expression of IL1β and inhibited this positive feedback loop of classical NF-κB (Fig. 4 H). Protein kinase C (PKC) could regulate the NF-κB and MAPK pathways, and the low prkcb (PKC) gene expression in GH-CDs further supported the possibility that GH-CDs may inhibit the activation of these two pathways, thus exerting an anti-inflammatory effect (Fig. 4 G-H). Besides, GH-CDs reduced the expression of several interleukins, colony-stimulating factors, and their receptors, which participated in inhibiting the JAK-STAT pathway to prevent inflammation progression (Fig. 4 I). CD19 is a B-cell surface marker with an important role in B-cell antigen receptor ligation-induced phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (PI3K) binding and activation, and its expression is reduced by GH-CDs [35]. The other downregulated DEGs in the PI3K-Akt pathway also demonstrated the ability of GH-CDs to inhibit PI3K activation by suppressing the expression of genes upstream of the pathway, such as several receptor tyrosine kinases, cytokines, and their receptors (Fig. 4 J). Taken together, these findings clarify the underlying mechanisms responsible for the anti-inflammatory effects of GH-CDs by inhibiting the TNF, MAPK, PI3K-Akt, JAK-STAT, and NF-κB signaling pathways.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo distribution of GH-CDs in mice
ce:section.ce:para:
To study the pharmacokinetics of GH-CDs in mice, the GH-CDs labeled with the fluorescent molecule Cy5.5 (GH-CDs-Cy5.5) were injected intraperitoneally into experimental mice. The distribution of GH-CDs-Cy5.5 in vivo and major organs were respectively examined by NIR in vivo small animal imager at 5 min, 10 min, 20 min, 40 min, 1.5 h, 3 h, 6 h, and 12 h after injection. Since there are differences in the internal environment between normal and colitis mice due to the inflammatory state, we further compared the distribution of GH-CDs in normal and colitis mice. The in vivo fluorescence intensity initially rose, then fell over time, peaking at approximately 1.5 h ( Figure S8 ). The fluorescence intensity of the in vivo and ex vivo organs decreased significantly at 12 h, avoiding toxicity due to drug accumulation (Fig. 5
A-C). Compared with normal mice, the colon fluorescence intensity in colitis mice was notably higher at 6 h (Fig. 5 D), which might be attributed to electrostatic interactions between electronegative GH-CDs and positively charged proteins that accumulate at sites of colonic inflammation[36], indicating that GH-CDs showed stronger retention ability in colitis mice and thus could better exert anti-inflammatory effects.

Drug safety is an important consideration for clinical applications and for this reason, we systematically evaluated the biocompatibility of GH-CDs. First of all, glucose and D-histidine as raw materials for the synthesis of GH-CDs are safe for humans. The MTT assay was utilized to assess in vitro biosafety, which showed that both RAW 264.7 and FHC cells maintained over 80 % viability after 24 h or 48 h of incubation with 800 μg/mL GH-CDs (Fig. 6
A-B). In Caco-2 cells, the biocompatibility of GH-CDs was equally excellent ( Figure S9 ). Next, hemolysis experiments were performed to investigate its hemocompatibility. After the concentration of 200 μg/mL GH-CDs was added to erythrocytes at 37 ℃ for 4 h, the hemolysis rate was only 1.39 % (Fig. 6 C).

Mice were intraperitoneally injected with 30 mg/kg GH-CDs for 7 consecutive days for in vivo biocompatibility experiments. Throughout the experiment, body weight changes in the control and GH-CDs groups were recorded daily (Fig. 6 D). After 7 d, major organs and blood samples were gathered for subsequent study. As shown in Fig. 6 E, we utilized H&E staining to assess the pathological damages, which showed that GH-CDs did not result in any significant pathological damage to vital organs. No notable variation was observed across the two groups when comparing routine blood indexes (Fig. 6 F-K), and major organ function indexes (Fig. 6 L-O). To summarize, GH-CDs are considered biocompatible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The therapeutic effect of GH-CDs on DSS-induced colitis
ce:section.ce:para:
Inspired by the above findings of GH-CDs in vitro, we set out to evaluate their therapeutic potential in vivo by utilizing mouse models of colitis induced by DSS. Mice were administered water with 2.5 % DSS every day and observed daily for alterations in body weight and feces. On day 7 of mouse colitis modeling, almost all mice showed symptoms such as bloody stools and weight loss, indicating that the colitis models were successfully established. After modeling completion, we randomized them into four groups: the DSS group, the 10 mg/kg/d GH-CDs group, the 20 mg/kg/d GH-CDs group, and the 5-ASA group. After mice were treated separately with 5 consecutive days of drug administration (Fig. 7
A), following performing intestinal endoscopy, we sacrificed the mice and collected their colons for analysis.

Throughout the experimental period, we recorded the changes in body weight and feces and calculated disease activity index (DAI) scores (Fig. 7 B-C). During the first 7 days, the mice showed weight loss and elevated DAI. After treatment with the corresponding drugs in each treatment group, there were some improvements in both indicators compared to the DSS group, with 20 mg/kg GH-CDs being the most effective. Similar results were also seen in the colon length statistics (Fig. 7 D, I). Colon endoscopy findings in the DSS group showed reddened areas of inflammation and bleeding, whereas endoscopic findings in the 20 mg/kg GH-CDs group visualized remission of colitis inflammation (Fig. 7 E). H&E and Alcian blue staining images showed the best therapeutic effect with reduced inflammatory cell infiltration, increased goblet cells, and the minimal level of damage to crypt structures in the 20 mg/kg GH-CDs treatment group compared to DSS and other treatment groups (Fig. 7 F-G).

Considering the important role of ROS in UC, we started to explore the antioxidant role of GH-CDs in vivo. First, we used DCFH-DA and DHE for ROS labeling in the colon to observe the in vivo antioxidant effects of GH-CDs. The DSS group had a strong green/red fluorescence, indicating that its ROS level was elevated after colitis modeling, and all treatment groups showed different levels of reduction, with the 20 mg/kg GH-CDs group having the closest effect to the control group (Fig. 7 H, S10). Myeloperoxidase (MPO) catalyzes the production of potent ROS, whose activity perpetuates inflammation and leads to tissue damage [37]. Malondialdehyde (MDA), a product of lipid peroxidation, functions as an immune adjuvant associated with the promotion of autoimmunity and inflammation. As shown in Fig. 7 J-K, GH-CDs treatment significantly reduced MPO activity and MDA content, demonstrating an effect against oxidative stress damage.

We further evaluated the restorative effect of GH-CDs on the integrity of the intestinal barrier. Alcian blue staining showed that GH-CDs restored the number of goblet cells (Fig. 7 G), which could maintain intestinal homeostasis by secreting mucins and forming a mucus layer. We then determined the TJP levels such as Claudin-1, Occludin, and ZO-1 in colons, which showed TJPs were significantly higher in colons of GH-CDs-treated m ice (Fig. 7 L). Finally, we performed TUNEL staining of intestinal tissues, which showed that GH-CDs inhibited apoptosis of colon tissue cells (Fig. 7 M). All these results indicated that GH-CDs treatment was effective in restoring the integrity of the mechanical and chemical intestinal barrier in UC.

To assess intestinal inflammation extent, we studied how GH-CDs affect the modulation of macrophage phenotypes in colons by CD80 labeling (Fig. 7 N) and IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α mRNA levels (Fig. 7 O-Q), which suggested GH-CDs inhibited pro-inflammatory M1-type polarization of macrophages and reduced pro-inflammatory cytokine expression. In summary, GH-CDs can effectively alleviate the symptoms of colitis by reducing ROS levels, decreasing the M1-type macrophage infiltration, lowering the levels of the pro-inflammatory cytokine, and repairing intestinal mechanical and chemical barriers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Regulation of gut microbiota and metabolites in UC by CH-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
In addition to the above-mentioned roles of GH-CDs in gut barrier repair such as inhibition of intestinal apoptosis, restoration of TJPs expression, and protection of the intestinal mucosal layer, the intestinal microbiota is also a crucial element of the intestinal barrier and significantly contributes to the remission process of UC. Hence, we examined the impact of GH-CDs on gut microbiota regulation through 16S ribosomal RNA gene sequencing. Firstly, Principal Component Analysis (PCA) was used for beta diversity analysis, where the more similar the species composition of the samples was, then the closer they were to each other in the PCA plot, the results of which showed that there was a clear difference in the intestinal microbiota between the DSS and control groups, while the GH-CDs group had a more similar composition of the microbiota to the control group (Fig. 8
A). Based on the Amplicon Sequence Variants (ASV) abundance table for each group, the number of ASVs shared and endemic to each group was visualized using a Venn diagram (Fig. 8 B). As shown in Fig. 8 C, the family-level composition of gut microbiota indicated that DSS treatment significantly decreased the content of beneficial bacteria such as Bifidobacteriaceae, Lactobacillaceae, and Eggerthellaceae in comparison to the control group, whereas the decrease was ameliorated through treatment with GH-CDs. GH-CDs treatment reduced the increased levels of harmful or pathogenic bacteria such as Enterococcaceae, Neisseriaceae, and Deferribacteraceae in the DSS group. At the genus level, we similarly used heatmaps to depict the differential abundance of gut microbiota (Fig. 8 D). Differential abundance at the genus level showed that the DSS group increased conditionally pathogenic bacteria including Neisseria, Paraprevotella, and Enterococcus while decreasing the beneficial bacteria such as Bifidobacterium, Lactobacillus, Faecalibaculum, and Enterorhabdus, and that treatment with GH-CDs could help reverse the above changes. Given these results, we propose that GH-CDs may contribute to the relief of UC by modulating the intestinal microbiota.

The gut microbiota interacts with the host's metabolism and could be implicated in the onset, progression, or remission of UC, for which we further analyzed their feces by metabolomics [38]. First, we performed Partial Least Squares Discriminant Analysis (PLS-DA) on each group of metabolites, which showed that GH-CDs altered intestinal metabolite composition in colitis mice (Fig. 8 E-F). We then plotted volcanoes to comprehend the general distribution of differential metabolites, which indicated that in comparison to the DSS group, the GH-CDs treatment up-regulated 41 metabolites and down-regulated 30 metabolites (Fig. 8 G). Among them, we found that DSS treatment inhibited secondary bile acid biosynthesis by KEGG enrichment analysis, and this inhibition was significantly reversed by GH-CDs treatment (Fig. 8 H-J). Many studies have shown that the accumulation of secondary bile acids has therapeutic and preventive effects on colitis, by inhibiting inflammation and restoring the intestinal epithelial barrier [39–41]. Additionally, GSEA showed GH-CDs treatment promoted the biosynthesis of isoflavonoid (Fig. 8 K), a class of flavonoids with favorable antioxidant properties, and the GH-CDs group significantly elevated the levels of apigenin and calycosin metabolite in isoflavonoid biosynthesis (Fig. 8 L-M), which has been shown to have potential in IBD treatment [42,43]. The above results indicated that GH-CDs may contribute to the remission of colitis by regulating intestinal microbiota and promoting secondary bile acids and isoflavonoid biosynthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preventive effects of GH-CDs on DSS-induced colitis
ce:section.ce:para:
UC is a chronic, relapsing IBD that alternates between periods of remission and flare-ups, so it is equally important to prevent the recurrence of ulcerative colitis during periods of remission. As shown in Fig. 9
A, we established a prophylactic model of colitis, and medications were administered on days 1, 3, 5, and 7 of colitis modeling, and the colon was collected after euthanasia on the eighth day. Compared to the DSS group, the 20 mg/kg GH-CDs group showed significant improvement in weight change and DAI score (Fig. 9 B-C). Colon length and intestinal endoscopic performance also clearly indicated great improvement in the inflammatory profile of colitis in the GH-CDs-treated group (Fig. 9 D, E, H). The H&E and Alcian blue staining images in the colon showed that the colitis mice presented obvious alterations such as disruption of epithelial cell continuity, the disappearance of crypt gland structure, inflammatory cells infiltration, and the decreasing number of goblet cells, whereas the groups with 5-ASA and 20 mg/kg GH-CDs were significantly improved, except for the 10 mg/kg GH-CDs group, which still had part of the colon showing inflammatory cells infiltration and the decreasing number of goblet cells (Fig. 9 F-G). As illustrated in Fig. 9 I-J, in comparison to the DSS group, GH-CDs groups significantly reduced the MPO viability and MDA content of colon tissue homogenates, which showed GH-CDs had the potential to safeguard the colon against oxidative stress damage. RT-qPCR was utilized to measure pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α) levels, which likewise indicated that GH-CDs were effective in preventing the recurrence of UC (Fig. 9 K-M).

To summarize, our study has examined the potential of biocompatible carbon-dots nanozymes (GH-CDs) as a treatment for ulcerative colitis, showcasing their antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, intestinal mechanical barrier repairing, and intestinal microecology regulating properties. GH-CDs have demonstrated significant radical scavenging abilities, mitigating oxidative stress caused by excessive ROS in the inflamed colon, and have shown effectiveness in inhibiting inflammatory pathways. Following intraperitoneal injection, the negatively charged GH-CDs exhibit prolonged retention in the colons of colitis mice may attributed to electrostatic interactions, thereby optimizing their therapeutic efficacy. In the context of the colitis model, GH-CDs demonstrate effectiveness in ameliorating colitis symptoms by mitigating ROS levels, attenuating M1 phenotype macrophage infiltration, suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion, restoring intestinal mechanical barrier integrity, and modulating intestinal microbiota and metabolites, ultimately facilitating the comprehensive regulation of intestinal homeostasis.
Ethics approval and consent to participate
All animal experiments were performed following the Principles of Laboratory Animal Care and Guidelines of the Laboratory Animal Care Committee of Wenzhou Medical University.
